docs.title: Entropy
docs.text:
Entropy is a developer-first chaos engineering CLI for Docker-based microservices.

It is written in Go and shipped as a single binary so developers can quickly run resilience checks in local environments.

docs.title: What You Can Do
docs.text:
• Auto-discover services from docker-compose.yml and generate chaos.yaml
  • Run random chaos in a daemon with safety constraints
  • Run deterministic scenarios with probe, inject, and wait steps
  • Inject lifecycle, network, and resource faults into target containers
  • Generate topology and resilience analysis outputs with topology and doctor

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Use one of the following methods:

# Install latest binary with Go
go install github.com/ibrahimkizilarslan/entropy/cmd/entropy@latest

# Or clone and build
git clone https://github.com/ibrahimkizilarslan/entropy.git
cd entropy
go build -o entropy ./cmd/entropy

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# 1) Generate a chaos config from compose services
./entropy init

# 2) Run deterministic scenario
./entropy scenario run chaos-scenario.example.yaml

# 3) Or run background random chaos
./entropy start --detach
./entropy status
./entropy stop

docs.title: Next Docs
docs.text:
• Scenario DSL Reference
  • Random Chaos Engine
  • CLI Reference
  • Release Checklist

docs.title: CLI Reference
docs.text: Entropy is managed entirely through its Command Line Interface (CLI). Below is a comprehensive list of all commands and their functionalities.

docs.title: entropy init
docs.text:
Scans the current environment (Docker Compose or Kubernetes) and automatically generates a default chaos.yaml configuration file populated with the discovered services/deployments as targets.

  • Usage: entropy init
  • Options:
    • --runtime <docker|kubernetes>: Container runtime to use (default: docker).
    • --force, -f: Overwrite chaos.yaml if it exists.

docs.title: entropy start
docs.text:
Starts the background chaos engine using the random injection model defined in chaos.yaml. The engine will periodically inject faults based on the configured intervals and safety constraints.

  • Usage: entropy start [OPTIONS]
  • Options:
    • --config <path>: Path to a custom configuration file (default: chaos.yaml).
    • --runtime <docker|kubernetes>: Container runtime to use (default: docker).
    • --detach, -d: Run engine in background mode.
    • --dry-run: Override config and log actions without executing them.
    • --max-down <number>: Override safety.max_down.
    • --cooldown <seconds>: Override safety.cooldown.

docs.title: entropy stop
docs.text:
Stops the running background chaos engine. This will gracefully terminate the daemon process but it will not automatically revert active faults (use entropy cleanup for that).

  • Usage: entropy stop

docs.title: entropy status
docs.text:
Displays the current status of the background chaos engine, including whether it is running, its PID, and the target configuration it was started with.

  • Usage: entropy status

docs.title: entropy logs
docs.text:
Streams the logs generated by the background chaos engine in real-time. Useful for monitoring which faults are being injected and observing safety constraint violations.

  • Usage: entropy logs

docs.title: entropy cleanup
docs.text:
Emergency cleanup command. Reverts all active faults that Entropy might have injected. It removes network constraints (tc rules) and restores resource limits (CPU/Memory) across all targeted containers.

  • Usage: entropy cleanup

docs.title: entropy inject [action] [target]
docs.text:
Manually injects a single chaos action into a target container.

  • Usage: entropy inject [action] [target] [OPTIONS]
  • Arguments:
    • [action]: One of stop, restart, pause, delay, loss, limit_cpu, limit_memory.
    • [target]: Docker container/service name or Kubernetes deployment/pod name.
  • Options:
    • --runtime <docker|kubernetes>: Container runtime to use (default: docker).
    • --config, -c <path>: Config file path used for target allow-list checks.
    • --skip-validation: Skip target allow-list check.
    • --latency <ms>: Delay latency (for delay).
    • --jitter <ms>: Delay jitter (for delay).
    • --loss <percent>: Packet loss percentage (for loss).
    • --cpu <cores>: CPU quota value (for limit_cpu).
    • --memory <mb>: Memory limit in MB (for limit_memory).
    • --duration <seconds>: Auto-restore duration.

docs.title: entropy doctor
docs.text:
Analyzes local Docker Compose or Kubernetes topology for resilience risks. It specifically checks for: - SPOF: Services with less than 2 replicas. - Resource Limits: Missing CPU or Memory limits. - Recovery: Missing restart policies (Docker). - Observability: Missing Liveness/Readiness probes (Kubernetes) or Healthchecks (Docker). - Security: Containers running in privileged mode.

  • Usage: entropy doctor
  • Options:
    • --runtime <docker|kubernetes>: Container runtime to use (default: docker).

docs.title: entropy topology
docs.text:
Visualizes discovered topology and network blast radius.

  • Usage: entropy topology

docs.title: entropy completion
docs.text:
Generates shell completion scripts.

  • Usage: entropy completion [bash|zsh|fish|powershell]

docs.title: Scenario Commands
docs.text: The Scenario Engine allows for deterministic, hypothesis-driven testing rather than random chaos.

docs.title: entropy scenario run <file>
docs.text:
Executes a deterministic chaos scenario defined in a YAML file. It runs the steps sequentially, evaluates HTTP probes, and reports whether the scenario hypothesis passed or failed.

  • Usage: entropy scenario run <path/to/scenario.yaml>
  • Arguments:
    • <file>: The path to the scenario YAML file (e.g., chaos-scenario.example.yaml).
  • Options:
    • --runtime <docker|kubernetes>: Container runtime to use (default: docker).
    • --report <html|json>: Report output format (default: html).
    • --output <path>: Write scenario report to disk.

docs.title: entropy docker list
docs.text:
Lists all running Docker containers on the host machine. This is a utility command to verify Docker connectivity and see what containers are available to target.

  • Usage: entropy docker list

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text: Variable Description Default ENTROPY_K8S_NAMESPACE Target namespace for Kubernetes operations. default ENTROPY_NET_INTERFACE The network interface name inside containers where tc rules are applied. eth0 KUBECONFIG Path to the kubeconfig file for cluster access. ~/.kube/config

docs.title: Random Chaos Engine
docs.text:
In addition to deterministic scenarios, Entropy features a Random Chaos Engine designed to act like a traditional Chaos Monkey. It runs in the background as a daemon and randomly targets services in your environment based on a predefined configuration.

This is ideal for continuous background testing while developing, ensuring that your code is resilient to unexpected blips.

docs.title: Configuration (chaos.yaml)
docs.text:
The Random Chaos Engine is driven by the chaos.yaml file (which can be auto-generated using entropy init).

# How often (seconds) to select and inject a chaos action
interval: 10

# Docker container names or docker-compose service names to target
targets:
  - service-a
  - service-b

# Actions to apply
actions:
  - stop
  - restart
  - pause

# Safety settings
safety:
  max_down: 1          # Maximum containers stopped simultaneously
  cooldown: 30         # Seconds to wait between injections
  dry_run: false       # If true, log actions without executing them

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Start the Engine: Run entropy start for foreground mode, or entropy start --detach to run in the background.
  2. Action Selection: Every interval seconds, the engine wakes up.
  3. Safety Check: The engine evaluates its safety rules:
    • Is it still within the cooldown period from the last injection? If yes, it sleeps.
    • Would stopping another container exceed max_down? If yes, it skips destructive lifecycle actions.
  4. Execution: If safety checks pass, it randomly selects a target from targets and an action from actions. It then applies the fault.
  5. Revert: If the action has a duration (like network latency or resource limits), Entropy automatically schedules a background task to revert the fault after the duration expires.
  6. Monitoring: You can view what the engine is doing by running entropy logs.

docs.title: Safety Mechanisms
docs.text:
Entropy is designed for local development, meaning it must be careful not to destroy your development environment completely.

  • max_down: This is a critical safety constraint. It tracks the state of all targeted containers. If max_down is set to 1, and service-a is currently stopped, Entropy will not stop service-b until service-a recovers. This prevents total system collapse.
  • cooldown: Ensures there is a breathing period between faults. If an action is injected, the engine will not inject another fault until the cooldown period has passed, giving orchestrators (like Docker Swarm or Kubernetes in future versions) time to react and recover.
  • Automatic Rollback: For temporary faults like CPU throttling or network latency, the engine tracks the injected state and guarantees it will be rolled back after the configured duration. If the engine crashes, entropy cleanup can be run to manually revert these states.

docs.title: Release Checklist
docs.text: Use this checklist before publishing a new release and announcing it publicly.

docs.title: 1) Repository Health
docs.text:
• [ ] Default branch is main
  • [ ] Repository visibility is set as intended (public/private)
  • [ ] README badges resolve successfully
  • [ ] README.md, CONTRIBUTING.md, CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md, LICENSE, SECURITY.md are present

docs.title: 2) CI Verification
docs.text:
• [ ] CI pipeline passes on main
  • [ ] go vet ./... passes
  • [ ] go test ./... passes
  • [ ] End-to-end smoke job passes in CI

docs.title: 3) Local Validation
docs.text:
• [ ] go build -o entropy ./cmd/entropy
  • [ ] ./entropy --help works
  • [ ] ./scripts/e2e-smoke.sh passes locally
  • [ ] ./scripts/e2e-smoke.sh --with-demo-compose passes locally

docs.title: 4) Documentation Accuracy
docs.text:
• [ ] Commands in README match actual CLI behavior
  • [ ] chaos.example.yaml is current
  • [ ] chaos-scenario.example.yaml is current
  • [ ] Docs under docs/ reflect latest command set and features

docs.title: 5) Release Preparation
docs.text:
• [ ] CHANGELOG.md updated under [Unreleased]
  • [ ] Version tag selected (for example v0.1.0)
  • [ ] Release notes drafted from changelog entries

docs.title: 6) Publish
docs.text:
• [ ] Create and push tag:
  • git tag vX.Y.Z
  • git push origin vX.Y.Z
  • [ ] Verify GitHub Release workflow completed
  • [ ] Verify release assets exist for all target platforms

docs.title: 7) Post-Release
docs.text:
• [ ] Validate installation from release assets
  • [ ] Announce release (LinkedIn/Reddit/GitHub)
  • [ ] Open follow-up issues for known gaps or roadmap items

docs.title: Scenario DSL Reference
docs.text: Entropy's Scenario Engine allows you to define deterministic, hypothesis-driven chaos experiments. Instead of randomly injecting faults, you can write step-by-step scenarios to test exactly how your system reacts to specific failures.

docs.title: Scenario File Structure
docs.text:
A scenario is defined in a YAML file (e.g., chaos-scenario.yaml) and consists of a name, description, hypothesis, and a list of sequential steps.

name: "Scenario Name"
description: "What this scenario tests"
hypothesis: "What should happen if the system is resilient"
steps:
  - # Step 1
  - # Step 2

docs.title: Step Types
docs.text: Entropy supports three types of steps: probe, inject, and wait.

docs.title: 1. Probe Step
docs.text:
Used to check the state of the system before, during, or after an injection. Entropy supports http, tcp, and exec probes.

- probe:
    type: http
    url: "http://localhost:8080/health"
    expect_status: 200        # Optional: Expected HTTP status code
    expect_not_status: 500    # Optional: Status code that should NOT be returned
    timeout: 5                # Optional: Timeout in seconds

docs.title: 2. Inject Step
docs.text:
Injects a specific fault into a target container. This can be a Docker lifecycle action, a network fault, or a resource constraint.

- inject:
    target: "my-service"      # The name of the Docker container or service
    action:
      name: "stop"            # The fault to inject (see Action Types below)

# Shorthand is supported when no action parameters are needed:
- inject:
    target: "my-service"
    action: stop

Action Types:

  • Lifecycle Actions:

    • stop: Stops the target container.
    • restart: Restarts the target container.
    • pause: Pauses the target container processes.

  • Network Actions (Requires tc and netem in the container):

    • delay: Injects network latency. yaml action: name: "delay" latency_ms: 300 jitter_ms: 50 duration: 30 # Seconds
    • loss: Drops a percentage of network packets. yaml action: name: "loss" percent: 20 duration: 30

  • Resource Constraints:

    • limit_cpu: Restricts CPU usage. yaml action: name: "limit_cpu" cpus: 0.5 # Limit to 0.5 cores duration: 60
    • limit_memory: Restricts Memory usage. yaml action: name: "limit_memory" memory_mb: 256 # Limit to 256 MB duration: 60

docs.title: 3. Wait Step
docs.text:
Pauses the scenario execution for a specified duration to allow state to propagate (e.g., waiting for an orchestrator to spin up a replacement container, or for a database connection to timeout).

- wait: 5s   # Supports formats like '5s', '1m', '10' (defaults to seconds)

docs.title: Running a Scenario
docs.text:
To execute a scenario, use the entropy scenario run command:

entropy scenario run path/to/chaos-scenario.yaml

Entropy will execute each step sequentially. If a probe step fails, the scenario will abort, indicating that the hypothesis failed. If all steps complete successfully, the hypothesis is validated.
